text: “I am under my families control and they are the one who are deciding for me.”
comments: Direct statement that family decides for her, confirming restricted decision-making.

text: “if it comes to marriage even if I chose by myself they have the power to stop my decision.”
comments: Family can override her marriage choice, confirming restricted agency.

text: “I have almost no freedom to decide by myself.”
comments: Very explicit statement of low autonomy.

text: “my father and my brothers decided for me sometimes they even restricted my movements.”
comments: Direct evidence of mobility restrictions by male relatives.

text: “when it comes to finance he is the one who decide… I just accept his decision.”
comments: Married woman describes husband as final financial decision-maker.

text: “education is not important for a girl, because you will get married to somebody else soon.”
comments: Father explicitly devalues girls’ education because of marriage.

text: “he is not able to invest in my education anymore because I am going anyway.”
comments: Reflects lower parental investment in daughters because they marry out.

text: “I can decide on other things such as when I want to sleep, work or not work in the house.”
comments: Nuances agency restriction by noting minor daily decisions remain hers.

text: “since I am married I and my husband discuss on our issues.”
comments: Nuanced case where wife reports some discussion with husband.

text: “my sisters want to study in XX and discussed with the family now they joined private collage.”
comments: Shows aspiration for further education and some family support.

text: “I finished 10th grade and forced to drop out of school because I married early (17 years) and get pregnant.”
comments: Clear aspiration-attainment gap due to early marriage and pregnancy.

text: “I really regret it.”
comments: Direct statement of regret indicates perceived gap between aspiration and life outcome.

text: “if I did not drop out… I would go to some place either as a nurse or teacher.”
comments: Clear occupational aspiration stated retrospectively.

text: “I want to be a doctor or an engineer.”
comments: Strong direct occupational aspiration.

text: “I want to help my younger sisters and to get better income.”
comments: Aspiration tied to income improvement and family support.

text: “I want my family to live a better life.”
comments: Broad aspiration for upward mobility and improved living conditions.

text: “most of the girls dropped out of school early.”
comments: Community-level evidence of limited female attainment.

text: “some cases they prefer male child to finish school.”
comments: Direct indication of unequal educational preference favoring boys.

text: “what good you bring to the family you will get married and you go somewhere else to serve others not us.”
comments: Reflects norm that daughters marry out and serve husband’s family, reducing value of schooling.

text: “Education should not be limited one should go as far as his/her capacity allow.”
comments: Normative aspiration for unrestricted education.

text: “girls dropped out from school because of marriage.”
comments: Directly links marriage to lower educational attainment for girls.

text: “girls eloping from school without their parents’ permission.”
comments: Elopement disrupts schooling and widens aspiration-attainment gap.

text: “some are forced to get married by the family.”
comments: Family-forced marriage restricts girls’ agency over schooling and life course.

text: “parents are freaked out and decided their girl children not to go to school.”
comments: Parents withdraw girls from school due to fear of elopement.

text: “they said she is a shame for them and not accept her.”
comments: Social stigma attached to girls who elope and return.

text: “you will be like her if I send you to school.”
comments: One girl’s elopement becomes a warning used to restrict others.

text: “school for girls become as a place of misbehaving.”
comments: Reflects gendered suspicion of girls’ schooling.

text: “Boys… tricked the girls… but it is always the girl to be blamed.”
comments: Girls are blamed more heavily than boys for romantic transgressions.

text: “I also help my mother in the household chores and in the farm.”
comments: Girls’ agricultural learning is embedded within domestic labor.

text: “I help her fetching water and collecting firewood.”
comments: Clear gendered division of labor in household provisioning tasks.

text: “boys unless they are very young are not doing it.”
comments: Contrasts girls’ domestic work with boys’ exemption.

text: “House hold workload”
comments: Domestic workload identified as a key school-ending factor.

text: “I met my husband… I thought it is better and eloped with him.”
comments: Elopement directly linked to failure to continue schooling.

text: “now I see my mistake and try to get out of my marriage.”
comments: Retrospective regret signals a strong aspiration-attainment gap.

text: “Girls… face lots of problem because of workload.”
comments: Heavy workload is identified as a major barrier to continued schooling.

text: “tricked by elder men… becoming second or third wife.”
comments: Older men exploit girls and push them into unequal marriages.

text: “eloping and getting pregnant but not married.”
comments: Romantic/sexual relationships disrupt education without secure marriage outcome.

text: “My goal is to graduate and becoming a nurse.”
comments: Clear occupational aspiration toward nursing.

text: “I want to continue my education once I give birth.”
comments: Education aspiration is postponed by pregnancy.

text: “if my husband allow me… if he said no I won’t go it is his will.”
comments: Husband has decisive authority over her continued education.

text: “I want to be a teacher.”
comments: Clear professional aspiration.

text: “I want to study English and to be a doctor.”
comments: Education seen as pathway to mobility and professional work.

text: “language is important to go other areas for work.”
comments: Links language skills to future employment mobility.

text: “my wish is to be an accountant to do my own business.”
comments: Explicit business-oriented aspiration, coded with ASP_BUSS.

text: “I want to be scientist and become well known.”
comments: High-status professional aspiration.

text: “some get married, there are some girls who go to XX.”
comments: Marriage and migration structure post-school trajectories.

text: “Here we see no hope.”
comments: Strong statement of limited local opportunities after schooling.

text: “some may start small trading businesses in the local market.”
comments: Trading is identified as a realistic post-school livelihood.

text: “girls stay home helping our mothers.”
comments: Reaffirms gendered domestic role after schooling ends.

text: “Most of us want to study until we can otherwise we join vocational school in XX.”
comments: Strong aspiration to continue schooling or move into vocational education.

text: “Going to XX if they afford it here there is nothing.”
comments: Education aspiration is constrained by affordability and lack of local options.

text: “Inset… and Coffee are the most important products for women.”
comments: Agricultural products are explicitly gendered for women.

text: “The sell from both the products also help us to cover the day to day household consumption.”
comments: Women’s products also function as small-scale income sources.

text: “Men prefer row planting and improved seeds to get more crop and production and income.”
comments: Men are linked with productivity-oriented innovation and income generation.

text: “both have the opportunity to learn but… men who are closer to the agriculture extension agent… are selected.”
comments: Opportunity exists in principle, but access is skewed toward connected men.

text: “one woman… did not know how many times to plow… she was waiting the whole day.”
comments: Example of a woman left without timely extension support.

text: “young men with land are the one who learn fast.”
comments: Land access supports faster learning and innovation uptake; women often lack it.

text: “agriculture extension agents prefer to approach rich and influential men not the poor and women.”
comments: Direct statement of gendered and class-based information access bias.

text: “land belongs to the man.”
comments: Explicit statement that ownership remains male even if work is shared.

text: “the vegetable garden is considered to be for women and the farm field is always for a man.”
comments: Strong evidence of gendered division of farming space.

text: “women in the house work in both lands.”
comments: Shows women’s labor burden spans both female and male plots.

text: “Women allowed to farm in her backyard… men very rarely help them.”
comments: Women’s garden space is acknowledged but male support is limited.

text: “women work with their husband in the farm while doing the house work and working on the backyard.”
comments: Clear statement of women’s multiple work burdens.

text: “if the vegetables are produced in large quantities he will sell it.”
comments: Male control increases when production has larger market value.

text: “Women always work in the husband’s plots starting from plowing.”
comments: Shows women’s labor contribution to men’s plots is constant and extensive.

text: “Now a day’s young couples work equally in their land.”
comments: Nuances gender division by suggesting some couples share work more equally.

text: “If it is in bulk they (men) sell it while it is in the plot.”
comments: Larger-scale sales move under men’s control.

text: “Men are not selling small amount of vegetable in the market… it is a shame.”
comments: Clear stigma against men doing female-coded petty selling.

text: “He will never allow her to join the group because she knows nothing about the market.”
comments: Women are denied market group participation on grounds of ignorance.

text: “if she asks him by herself he will never allow her.”
comments: Husband is framed as gatekeeper over women’s association.

text: “if she refused him and join the group for sure he will throw her out and marry another one.”
comments: Sanctions for defiance include expulsion and polygyny.

text: “as far as he is the one controlling the money.”
comments: Husband’s control of income constrains women’s market autonomy.

text: “If she refuses to give him the income that’s the end of their marriage.”
comments: Economic disobedience is framed as grounds for marital dissolution.

text: “He may even becoming violence and starts thinking that she saw someone else.”
comments: Autonomy is sexualized and may provoke violence.

text: “Men never think women need rest.”
comments: Normalizes women’s overwork and lack of rest.

text: “He will keep on asking her to join him in the farm while she is working hard in her backyard and household.”
comments: Strong statement of women’s triple labor burden.

text: “a man under his wife’s wing is the weakest and controlled type.”
comments: Strong stigma against men perceived as subordinated to wives.

text: “he is not a man.”
comments: Masculinity is denied when household roles are reversed.

text: “She will be alienated and insulted by fellow women friends and his family.”
comments: Norm-breaking women face social exclusion from both women and family.

text: “She might be considered as greedy, prostitute.”
comments: Severe sexual/moral stigma attached to market-active women.

text: “No one respected him.”
comments: Male household role reversal produces social disrespect.

text: “they will call him names or fight with him for no reason.”
comments: Social sanctions against the man are verbal and physical.

text: “Men will ask him not to be with such a woman and… marry another decent woman.”
comments: Community pressure encourages reassertion of conventional gender order.

text: “He would be proud… because he allows his wife to work.”
comments: One respondent imagines a supportive response to female economic success.

text: “he will feel inferior to her and act like a man.”
comments: Community pressure makes men reassert masculinity if wife succeeds.

text: “Both men and women get things equally.”
comments: Basic affirmative definition of equality.

text: “Both men and women are equal in which the man will not think that women are subordinate.”
comments: Direct statement opposing women’s subordination.

text: “if a woman is talking in a meeting she is considered talkative and if it is a man he is considered wise.”
comments: Public speech is evaluated differently for women and men.

text: “our family sends both boys and girls to school.”
comments: Indicates perceived improvement in educational equality.

text: “there is no equality in our area all the decision is for men.”
comments: Strong direct rejection of the claim that equality exists.

text: “All the decision is made in consultation with the husband and wife.”
comments: Presents a cooperative ideal of marital decision-making.

text: “my father is the one who decide at the end, my mother may oppose but… she accept whatever he said.”
comments: Even in a ‘consultative’ frame, men retain final authority.

text: “She will never decide by herself… to sell it’s his decision.”
comments: Confirms that sale decisions, especially for larger quantities, belong to men.

text: “If it’s small garden… it is fully her decision.”
comments: Nuances the norm by showing women’s limited autonomy over small-scale produce.

text: “She will sale if it is not in bulk, if it is in big amount the man will sale it.”
comments: Women may sell small quantities, but men control bulk sales.

text: “selling small amount of vegetable in the market is a shame for the man.”
comments: Clear stigma against men doing women-coded petty marketing.

text: “all will laugh at him and insult him; no one will buy from him.”
comments: Strong social sanction for men crossing into women’s selling role.

text: “If the idea comes from her he will never accept it.”
comments: Women’s ideas are discounted when they originate from wives.

text: “secretly he will go to agriculture extension agents… as if the idea is his own.”
comments: Male appropriation of women’s knowledge confirms unequal recognition.

text: “If he is open minded he will listen and try it in small land first.”
comments: Nuances norm by allowing some conditional receptiveness.

text: “She will be afraid if she fails… He can beat her.”
comments: Risk-taking is constrained by threat of blame and violence.

text: “She knows she cannot do whatever she can.”
comments: Direct statement of restricted female autonomy in asset use.

text: “He also will challenge her or trick her to get the money for himself.”
comments: Husband is expected to manipulate or override her control over inheritance.

text: “the money is inherited it is her money even the law supports her.”
comments: Nuances norm by referencing legal support for women’s ownership.

text: “at the end this all will be the source of violence and even killing.”
comments: Even with legal backing, social enforcement can be violent.

text: “It is very easy for him he is a man and the money is his.”
comments: Men are seen as having unquestioned authority over inherited assets.

text: “he will tell her it is none of your business.”
comments: Wives are excluded from men’s asset decisions.

text: “if a woman has young kids she is not allowed to go to the market.”
comments: Motherhood is used to justify restricting mobility.

text: “she is expected to cook, to raise the kids.”
comments: Domestic and childcare roles underpin mobility restrictions.

text: “Women are moving from place to place if they go in a group or with men.”
comments: Women’s mobility is conditional on accompaniment.

text: “if women walk after 6:00 Pm it is dangerous and open for violence.”
comments: Safety threats reinforce mobility limits.

text: “She can walk freely to the market but walking around is a taboo.”
comments: Distinguishes acceptable purposeful mobility from stigmatized free movement.

text: “It is easy for a female household head to sell vegetable and small amount of grain.”
comments: Nuances norm by showing widowed/female heads can sell certain products.

text: “For women with a husband it is him who is going to market with big amount of grains… and big animals.”
comments: Husbands control larger-scale commercial sales.

text: “it is not always safe… there is also abduction and rape.”
comments: Violence risk is a clear practical mobility constraint.

text: “If married it is her husband if not her parents and elder brother who decide on her movement.”
comments: Female mobility is governed by male authority across the life course.

text: “if women are in a group they can go where ever they want.”
comments: Nuances mobility restriction by showing some freedom in groups.

text: “Woman walking alone sometimes may get attacked by men and raped.”
comments: Clear safety-based restriction on women’s independent mobility.

text: “there are girls who finished school and go to other places to seek job.”
comments: Young women migrate for employment after schooling.

text: “some family… send the girl child either to work for others or to relatives in the town for education purpose.”
comments: Girls are sent out for both work and education opportunities.

text: “girls who returned from XX with nothing… some are mentally ill… people feel sorry… some gossip.”
comments: Failed or troubled migration attracts pity and gossip.

text: “women who return with some money… open small shops in the near town.”
comments: Return migration can support women’s business formation.

text: “women who go to the nearest town for work as teachers, in hotels or shops.”
comments: Lists concrete occupational destinations for women migrants.

text: “girls start to marry 15-17 and for boys it is 24.”
comments: Strong gender age gap in marriage timing.

text: “easy for the men to get young girls in school and trick them then eloping… become co-wife.”
comments: School-going girls are vulnerable to manipulation into marriage/co-wife status.

text: “my father told me I am very young to be a second wife I said no and got married.”
comments: Marriage negotiations involve co-wife status and parental resistance, but outcomes remain risky.

text: “marriage is arranged between the boy and the girl, on their own will.”
comments: Nuances marriage norms by noting some self-choice marriages.

text: “there is marriage among families without the knowledge of the girl.”
comments: Confirms persistence of arrangements excluding girls’ consent.

text: “Men always know whom they marry and they are the one who chose.”
comments: Men’s marriage agency is greater and more secure than women’s.

text: “I prefer if it is in agreement between the boy and the girl based on love.”
comments: Expresses support for equal and consensual marriage decisions.

text: “women who gave birth when they are 13-14 (either married off by the family or raped).”
comments: Early first birth results from coercive marriage or sexual violence.

text: “mostly in our area we give birth when we reach at the age of 17-18.”
comments: Indicates socially expected early first childbirth.

text: “this age is not a good age to have baby… the mother is not yet settled and her body is not still matured.”
comments: Respondent explicitly rejects early motherhood as harmful.

text: “If a girl marry at young age she forced to stop her education.”
comments: Early marriage directly blocks attainment of educational aspiration.

text: “carried heavy responsibility of the house, and kids are coming unplanned.”
comments: Early marriage shifts girls quickly into domestic and reproductive roles.

text: “When she is mature at least reached 20-23 years of age.”
comments: Supports later, more mature timing for first birth.

text: “once you get married all expect you to have kids.”
comments: Social expectation removes women’s reproductive timing choice.

text: “if not the husband start looking for other option or get married again.”
comments: Women are pressured into early childbirth to retain husbands.

text: “women… just get pregnant whenever the husband want.”
comments: Husband preference dominates fertility timing for many women.

text: “if they stops there is violence or he gets married to another one.”
comments: Sanctions for women’s reproductive control include violence and polygyny.

text: “Women are taking birth control but most of them hide from their husband.”
comments: Concealed contraception signals lack of open reproductive agency.

text: “Some men even threaten the women if she doesn’t give him more children he will leave her.”
comments: Threat of abandonment enforces women’s fertility compliance.

text: “Both the women and the husband are not deciding… it is God’s will.”
comments: Nuances agency discussion by invoking divine will rather than individual choice.

text: “I wish girls are stop marrying at early age and pursue their education.”
comments: Desired change centers girls’ continued education instead of early marriage.

text: “Having further education in our area; and work opportunity for all.”
comments: Broad aspiration for education access and employment opportunity.
